title: Reuse-based wide temporaries optimization
body:
This PR introduces the new `V3ReuseWideTemps` stage which minimizes number of wide temps by reusing them across their references.

This optimization significantly shortens compilation times. Gains are visible for designs with tracing enabled and modules that contain large number of variables.
This is especially visible on Chisel generated designs.


## Rationale

Currently, Verilator contains two phases that accomplish this effect: V3Expand + V3Subst. However, V3Expand only affects variables whose width fits within expand limit.
Unfortunately, increasing expand limit radically enlarges memory usage (as mentioned in [#3005](https://github.com/verilator/verilator/issues/3005)) thus V3Subst, which works on expanded nodes into `AstWordSel`s, does not optimize all temporary variables leading to the bloated number of wide temporaries.

The `V3ReuseWideTemps` aims to optimize all temps, regardless their size, while not increasing verilation memory usage nor duration.


## Benchmarks

Consider the following Chisel code:
```scala
package xor

import chisel3._
import _root_.circt.stage.ChiselStage

class XOR extends Module {
  val VARS = 32768
  val WIDTH = 1
  val in = IO(Input(UInt(16.W)))
  val out = IO(Output(UInt(WIDTH.W)))

  val test = RegInit(VecInit(Seq.fill(VARS)(1.U(WIDTH.W))))

  for (i <- test.indices) {
    test(i) := test(i) ^ in
  }

  out := test(in)
}

object XOR extends App {
  ChiselStage.emitSystemVerilogFile(
    new XOR,
    firtoolOpts = Array(
      "-disable-all-randomization",
      "-strip-debug-info"
      // "--preserve-aggregate=all"
    )
  )
}
```
Generated file: [XOR.sv.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17502979/XOR.sv.zip)
Compilation of generated SystemVerilog file was performed with the following flags:

```bash
--trace --binary --cc
```

Compiler: `clang 18.1.8`


Verilation + compilation without optimization:

```
- V e r i l a t i o n   R e p o r t: Verilator 5.029 devel rev v5.028-250-g75c45a6fb
- Verilator: Built from 3.994 MB sources in 2 modules, into 23.404 MB in 38 C++ files needing 255.580 MB
- Verilator: Walltime 319.148 s (elab=1.105, cvt=7.997, bld=288.987); cpu 30.104 s on 1 threads; alloced 1298.656 MB
```

Verilation + compilation with optimization:

```
- V e r i l a t i o n   R e p o r t: Verilator 5.029 devel rev v5.028-250-g75c45a6fb
- Verilator: Built from 3.994 MB sources in 2 modules, into 23.362 MB in 38 C++ files needing 0.059 MB
- Verilator: Walltime 173.210 s (elab=1.216, cvt=7.536, bld=142.967); cpu 30.154 s on 1 threads; alloced 415.656 MB

```

The gains are even more noticeable when `--output-split-*` options are used in the real world designs with huge number of `reg`s. This optimization made it possible to compile them.


## Algorithm preconditions

The `V3ReuseWideTemps` is launched after `V3Premit` stage, which adds wide temporaries with prefix `__Vtemp`. Since, each temporary is used exactly twice; for one `WRITE` and one `READ`, they are in a Static Single-assignment form.


## Algorithm

Each temporary is replaced with the widest free one. Temporaries are considered free if they are unused or a `READ` was the last operation performed on them. If there are no free temporary, artificial one is created.


## The small design case

Unfortunately, for a small number of variables, this optimization emits more temporaries than when it is disabled. For instance, in case of operation that takes 4 temporaries as arguments (ex. inherits from AstNodeQuadop) minimal number of temporaries after optimization is 4 which cannot be further reduced by V3Expand and V3Subst. Thus a couple more temporaries are emitted than in the case of V3Expand + V3Subst on increased `expand-limit`.

@wsnyder I added `-fno-wide-temp-opt` flag, but I presume that it is not desired to generate more temporaries for small designs. Should this optimization be optional?
